items.q: How does the transit method detect an exoplanet?
items.correct: By the small dip in a star's brightness as the planet passes in front of it.
items.misconception: By directly photographing the planet's surface.
items.random_wrong: By picking up the planet's radio signals.
items.why: Transits cause a tiny, regular dip in starlight that reveals a planet crossing the star's disc.

items.q: What is the 'habitable zone' around a star?
items.correct: The range of distances where liquid water could exist on a planet's surface.
items.misconception: The zone where aliens definitely live.
items.random_wrong: The region inside the star itself.
items.why: The habitable zone is defined by temperature for liquid water, not by any guarantee of life.

items.q: Does the radial velocity method see the planet directly?
items.correct: No - it detects the star's tiny wobble caused by the planet's gravity.
items.random_wrong: It measures the planet's surface temperature.
items.why: Radial velocity infers a planet from the Doppler shift of the star's light as it wobbles.
The answer examiners want is: Transit photometry. “Direct imaging” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Doppler shifts of the star. “Brightness dips” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Year of the first hot Jupiter discovery (51 Pegasi b)?
items.why: The real figure is 1995. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
